name: Seker
description: Seker, also spelled Sokar, is an ancient Egyptian funerary god, typically depicted as a falcon-headed man or a mummified man wearing the Atef crown. His origins are obscure, potentially pre-dating the unification of Egypt. He is primarily associated with the Memphite necropolis, specifically the area of Saqqara, which was known as Rostau. Seker embodies the mysteries of death, decay, and regeneration. He is a patron of craftsmen, particularly those involved in metalworking and the construction of tombs. His powers include control over the transition between life and death, the protection of the deceased in the afterlife, and the ability to grant resurrection. Seker's role is crucial in the journey of the soul through the underworld, guiding the deceased through the dangerous trials they face. He is often merged with other deities, such as Ptah and Osiris, forming syncretic deities like Ptah-Seker and Ptah-Seker-Osiris, reflecting the complex and evolving nature of Egyptian religious beliefs. These combined forms represent the creative power of Ptah, the funerary aspects of Seker, and the resurrection promise of Osiris, offering hope for eternal life after death. Seker's festivals, particularly those held during the month of Khoiak, involved elaborate processions and rituals symbolizing the rebirth of the sun and the deceased.
subtitle: God of Death, Decay, and Regeneration

keyMyths.title: Seker's Role in the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Seker guides the deceased through the dangerous trials of the underworld, protecting them from the many threats they face. He ensures their safe passage to the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: The Festival of Seker
keyMyths.description: During the month of Khoiak, elaborate festivals are held in Seker's honor, celebrating his power over death and resurrection. These festivals involve processions and rituals symbolizing rebirth.

keyMyths.title: The Union of Ptah-Seker-Osiris
keyMyths.description: Seker merges with Ptah and Osiris to form a syncretic deity, representing the creative power of Ptah, the funerary aspects of Seker, and the resurrection promise of Osiris. This union offers hope for eternal life.

sources.author: R. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: E. Hornung
sources.work: The Ancient Egyptian Books of the Afterlife
sources.description: Analysis of funerary texts and their significance.
sources.text: Analysis of funerary texts and their significance.

sources.author: G. Pinch
